questions.text: Where do you declare provider version requirements in Terraform 1.13?

options.text: In a providers.tf file at project root
In the terraform block using required_providers
In each resource block using provider_version
In .terraform.lock.hcl directly

questions.explanation: Provider version requirements go in the terraform {} block under required_providers. Each provider entry specifies source (registry path) and version (constraint). This replaces the older top-level provider version argument which is now deprecated.

questions.text: What does the version constraint '~> 5.0' mean?

options.text: Exactly version 5.0
Greater than 5.0
>= 5.0.0 and < 6.0.0 (allows minor and patch updates)
>= 5.0.0 and < 5.1.0 (allows only patch updates)

questions.explanation: The pessimistic constraint ~> 5.0 means >= 5.0 and < 6.0 (allows 5.x versions). ~> 5.0.0 would mean >= 5.0.0 and < 5.1.0 (allows only patch updates). The rightmost specified component determines the upper bound increment.

options.text: Stores the current state of infrastructure
Locks provider versions and checksums for reproducibility
Contains sensitive provider credentials
Prevents multiple users from running terraform simultaneously

questions.explanation: The dependency lock file (.terraform.lock.hcl) records the exact provider versions and checksums selected by terraform init. Committing this file ensures all team members and CI/CD pipelines use the same provider versions. Update it with terraform init -upgrade.

questions.text: How do you configure a secondary AWS provider for a different region?

options.text: provider "aws2" { region = "eu-west-1" }
provider "aws" { alias = "eu", region = "eu-west-1" }
provider "aws" { secondary_region = "eu-west-1" }
resource "aws_provider" "eu" { region = "eu-west-1" }

questions.explanation: Provider aliases allow multiple configurations of the same provider. Add 'alias = "name"' to the provider block. Reference it in resources with 'provider = aws.eu'. The default provider (no alias) is used when no provider argument is specified in a resource.

questions.text: What is the provider source format in required_providers?

options.text: provider/name
registry.terraform.io/namespace/name
namespace/name
terraform-provider-name

questions.explanation: The source in required_providers uses namespace/name format (e.g., hashicorp/aws, hashicorp/random). The full address is registry.terraform.io/namespace/name, but the hostname is optional for the default public registry. For private registries: myprivate.registry/namespace/name.

questions.text: How does the AWS provider authenticate by default when no credentials are in the provider block?

options.text: It prompts for credentials interactively
It checks environment variables, ~/.aws/credentials, instance profile (IAM role)
It uses a built-in demo account
It fails immediately if no credentials are in the provider block

questions.explanation: The AWS provider uses the standard AWS credential chain: environment variables (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY), shared credentials file (~/.aws/credentials), AWS IAM role via instance metadata (for EC2/ECS/Lambda), and more. Never hard-code credentials in provider blocks.

questions.text: Which command upgrades providers to the latest versions allowed by version constraints?

options.text: terraform upgrade providers
terraform init -upgrade
terraform providers update
terraform get -update

questions.explanation: terraform init -upgrade instructs Terraform to update the lock file to the latest provider versions that satisfy the version constraints. Without -upgrade, init reuses locked versions. After upgrading, commit the updated .terraform.lock.hcl.

questions.text: What does the 'terraform providers' command show?

options.text: Lists all available providers in the Terraform registry
Shows providers required by the current configuration and their versions
Displays provider documentation
Installs providers without initializing the backend

questions.explanation: terraform providers displays a tree of provider requirements for the current configuration, showing which modules require which providers and their version constraints. Use 'terraform providers lock' to update the lock file, and 'terraform providers mirror' to download providers for air-gapped use.

questions.text: What happens if you run 'terraform init' after adding a new provider to required_providers?

options.text: Nothing — you must run terraform apply to install new providers
Terraform downloads and installs the new provider plugin
An error is thrown because you must delete .terraform first
Terraform ignores the new provider until terraform validate is run

questions.explanation: Running terraform init after adding a provider to required_providers downloads and installs the new provider plugin into the .terraform/providers directory and updates the lock file. This is the correct workflow: add provider to required_providers, then run init.

questions.text: Which major feature reached General Availability (GA) in Terraform 1.13?

options.text: for_each support in standard provider blocks for multi-account deployments
terraform stacks — composing multiple configurations into one deployable unit with shared state coordination
Automatic provider version selection based on resources used
Built-in provider hot-reloading without terraform init

questions.explanation: Terraform 1.13 promoted the 'terraform stacks' CLI command to GA. Stacks allow you to compose multiple Terraform configurations into a single deployable unit with coordinated state management — ideal for platform teams managing multi-component infrastructure. Important note: for_each in provider blocks is a Terraform Stacks-only feature and is NOT available in standard open-source Terraform provider blocks.
